input_path: data\papers\Zech et al. - Model-driven Digital Twins for AECO.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: Infrastructure assets within the Architecture, Engineering, Construction, and Operations (AECO) domain, exemplified by buildings, characterized by the integration of static models with runtime data to create virtual representations for simulation, analysis, and optimization across the entire lifecycle from initial design to operational refurbishment and adaptations.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: Real-time monitoring through insights, predictive analytics for forecasting, operational optimization, enhanced decision-making capabilities, and improved stakeholder collaboration across the building lifecycle phases including design, construction, and operations.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange, leverages Unreal Engine for rich, interactive visualization, and is grounded in Building Information Modeling (BIM).
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: Multiple DT instances coordinated by a central DT Framework hub, with Building Control Engineers handling device modeling and deployment initiation, Facility Managers performing real-time monitoring and control, and DT Engineers extending capabilities through service integration
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: Modular architecture with standardized extension points for custom service integration; central DT Framework hub facilitating bidirectional data exchange and holistic building lifecycle management through coordinated roles
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: Building Information Modeling (BIM) is used for device modeling of sensors, actuators, and controllers, with Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) transforming enriched BIM models into structured digital twin artifacts. Real-time data exchange ensures synchronization between physical and digital components.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The framework integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange, enabling live synchronization between physical assets and digital twins through continuous data ingestion from building sensors and control systems.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Bidirectional communication mechanisms feed control signals back to Building Control Systems (BCS), allowing Facility Managers to interact with the deployed DT for real-time monitoring and control of physical assets.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The framework employs a layered system architecture coupling BIM with middleware for automated commissioning, integrating Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange, ensuring reliable and scalable connectivity between physical devices and the digital twin.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: A model-driven framework enables scalable DT deployment with automated processes that reduce manual intervention, supporting modular architecture with runtime configuration management for adjustments without full redeployment and scalability from individual components to entire infrastructure systems.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The Digital Twin lifecycle encompasses initial design (BIM-based device modeling by Building Control Engineers), deployment (automated artifact generation via Model-Driven Engineering), operational monitoring (real-time data synchronization and interactive visualization), and evolution (custom service integration). Transition between phases is managed through MDE-driven transformations from static BIM models to dynamic DTs, with governance enforced by role-specific responsibilities (Building Control Engineers, Facility Managers, DT Engineers) as per Table I.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The framework leverages Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) principles to extract structured representations from enriched BIM models and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention. A modular architecture featuring standardized extension points enables the integration of custom services and analytics, facilitating DT evolution in response to changing operational requirements.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: Real-time data exchange ensures continuous synchronization between physical and digital counterparts, with interactive visualization providing immersive interfaces for Facility Managers to monitor system states and operate infrastructure. Custom analytics services can be integrated through standardized extension points to support decision-making processes.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The framework adheres to AECO-specific standards, particularly Building Information Modeling (BIM), for initial device modeling and representation of building control system components. It implements standardized extension points to ensure interoperability and modular integration of custom services, aligning with industry requirements for DT system compatibility and data exchange protocols.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The description accurately synthesizes the definition and scope of the system found in the Introduction. It correctly identifies the domain (AECO), the asset type (buildings), the technical nature of the twin (integration of static models with runtime data), its purposes (simulation, analysis, optimization), and the lifecycle scope. Supported by quotes: 'cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static models with runtime data creates a virtual representation', 'cyber-physical asset (e.g., a building)', 'along the complete building life cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations'.

The extraction lists specific services mentioned in the text, including monitoring, analytics, decision support, and collaboration. It correctly attributes these to the building lifecycle context. Supported by quotes: 'real-time monitoring', 'predictive analytics', 'enhanced decision-making capabilities', and 'improved stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life cycle'.

The extraction precisely identifies the tools and technologies mentioned in the text and their specific roles: Eclipse Hono and Ditto for data exchange, Unreal Engine for visualization, and BIM as the foundation. Quotes: 'grounded in Building Information Modeling (BIM)', 'integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange', 'leverages Unreal Engine for rich, interactive visualization'.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

block_2_judge.reasoning: The extracted description accurately reflects the source text, which explicitly states, 'Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts' (Section III.A). Additionally, the abstract mentions 'real-time data exchange' via Eclipse Hono and Ditto. The description is specific and well-supported, lacking only deeper technical details about latency or synchronization frequencies not present in the text.

The description correctly identifies the central DT Framework hub and the roles of Building Control Engineers, Facility Managers, and DT Engineers. This is directly supported by Table I and Section III.A, which detail these roles. Section III.B also states, 'The DT Framework acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange.' However, the description misses potential details about the hierarchical structure or specific coordination mechanisms beyond roles, which are not deeply explored in the text.

The description accurately captures the modular architecture with standardized extension points, as stated in Section III.A: 'The framework’s modular architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the integration of custom services and analytics.' It also correctly notes the central DT Framework hub's role in bidirectional data exchange and holistic lifecycle management (Section III.B). The coordination of roles is well-supported by Table I and Figure 1. The description is comprehensive and well-grounded.

The description states 'Not in Document.' After reviewing the source text, there is no mention of integration with external systems, interoperability standards with other Digital Twins, or enterprise-level data exchange protocols beyond the internal framework components (Eclipse Hono, Ditto, Unreal Engine) and BIM models. Therefore, the claim is correct.

block_3_docs_retrieved:
TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

block_3_judge.reasoning: The description accurately summarizes the paper's core methodology and data architecture. It correctly identifies BIM as the modeling foundation for sensors and actuators ('define sensors, actuators, and controllers directly within the BIM environment'), the use of MDE for generating artifacts ('automates the extraction of domain semantics and the generation of runtime interfaces'), and the real-time synchronization mechanism ('integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange').

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The provided source text focuses on the framework architecture and the *modeling* of devices rather than describing specific physical acting components, their control capabilities, ranges, or safety constraints. While it mentions 'lighting systems' and 'actuators' in the context of modeling requirements, it does not provide the technical details required by the characteristic definition.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The source text mentions sensors in the context of 'post-occupancy sensor data' and 'define sensors... directly within the BIM environment', but it does not describe specific sensor types, measurement capabilities, sampling frequencies, or spatial distribution. The text focuses on the integration framework rather than the physical specifications of the sensors.

The document mentions 'measuring how well the resulting DT reflects its physical counterpart using the Overall Digital Twin Entanglement (ODTE) metric' and 'model-driven... transformation pipeline to generate consistent runtime artifacts', which relates to validity. However, it does not detail specific model fidelity requirements, uncertainty sources, or error detection/correction mechanisms. Stating 'Not in Document' is accurate as the text does not contain the depth required by the characteristic description.
quotes: measuring how well the resulting DT reflects its physical counterpart using the Overall Digital Twin Entanglement (ODTE) metric

block_4_judge.reasoning: The extraction correctly identifies the use of Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange and live synchronization. This is supported by the text: 'The framework integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange' and 'Real-time data integration using Eclipse Hono and Ditto, enabling live synchronization between physical assets and digital twins'. However, it lacks specific details on data types, transmission frequencies, or preprocessing steps mentioned in the characteristic description.

The extraction mentions bidirectional communication and control signals fed back to Building Control Systems (BCS), which aligns with the text: 'mechanisms to feed control signals back to the BCS' and 'bidirectional communication between virtual and physical assets'. It also correctly notes Facility Managers' interaction with the DT. However, it omits details on command types, validation, or conflict resolution.

The extraction accurately describes the layered system architecture coupling BIM with middleware and the integration of Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange. This is supported by: 'TwinLight proposes a layered system architecture that couples BIM with a middleware' and 'integrating with Eclipse Hono [15] and Ditto [16], our proposal supports real-time data exchange'. However, it lacks specifics on network topology, bandwidth, or security protocols.

The extraction provides a detailed and accurate description of the model-driven framework for scalable DT deployment, including automated processes, modular architecture, and runtime configuration management. This is strongly supported by multiple quotes: 'model-driven framework for scalable DT deployment', 'automates the extraction of domain semantics and the generation of runtime interfaces, thereby reducing manual effort', and 'modular architecture with defined extension points to support scalable, service-oriented DT development'.

block_6_docs_retrieved:
[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service
